footer.vueQueryTitle: Empties the TanStack Vue Query client cache
footer.redisTitle: Wipes the apod: namespace on the Redis server
footer.statusCleared: Redis cache cleared · next load is a cold start
footer.toastInvalidated: Vue Query cache invalidated — the next data page will refetch.
footer.toastRedisFail: Something went wrong. Redis cache was not cleared.

how.heading: How it works
how.leadBefore: A slow, rate-limited API — made to feel instant. Three caches sit between you and NASA, each responsible for a

steps.name: NASA APOD API
steps.desc: The single source of truth. Every real request is rate-limited and takes the better part of a second.

steps.name: Redis
steps.desc: Stores the normalized data for 24 hours, shared by every visitor to the site.

steps.name: Vue Query
steps.desc: Keeps a copy in the browser, so returning to a page renders instantly — no round-trip at all.

steps.name: Your browser
steps.desc: The HTTP cache and Netlify Image CDN deliver the pictures themselves — a separate layer from the data.

how.guideLead: The controls in the footer work on every page — here is what to watch.

guide.title: Read the badges
guide.desc: Each card and the hero show which layer served the data: the Vue Query mark for the client cache, the Redis mark for the server cache, or the NASA meatball for a fresh fetch.

guide.title: Invalidate Vue Query
guide.desc: Drops the browser copy and refetches — from Redis if it is still warm, otherwise straight from NASA. The footer reports the source and how long it took.

guide.title: Clear Redis
guide.desc: Wipes the server cache. The source badge flips to the NASA meatball and the next load is a real, rate-limited request — a genuine cold start.

about.heading: Making a slow API feel instant
about.lead1: A small side project about caching. The idea is simple: take NASA's Astronomy Picture of the Day, a lovely but slow and rate-limited API, and see how instant it can feel with a couple of caches in front of it.
about.lead2: The browser never calls NASA directly. A Nuxt server route fetches and normalises the data, Redis keeps it for 24 hours, and Vue Query holds a copy in your browser. Clear either cache from the footer and watch where the next request really goes.
about.creditText: Built by Melanie Stief — who has always dreamed of flying to space, or at least working at NASA (which, sadly, will probably never happen in this lifetime).
